study_metadata.title: Evaluating Safety Constraint Violations in LLM-Based Agents Under Adversarial Prompting

rq1_violation_rates_by_attack_type.description: RQ1: Constraint violation rates across attack categories (single-turn, tool-enabled agent)

results.notes: Highest violation rate among direct attack types; severity skews high when successful
Indirect injection via simulated tool output significantly outperforms direct injection
Lower rate but highest avg severity when successful; bimodal distribution (critical or miss)
Incremental scope expansion more effective than direct escalation demands
Developer/system impersonation most effective; user impersonation least
Helpfulness bias exploitable; agents framed as assistants fail more than role-constrained agents
Lowest violation rate; roleplay framing most effective technique
High severity when successful; tool-call parameter manipulation most reliable vector

rq2_interaction_length_effect.description: RQ2: Effect of interaction length on constraint violation rates (prompt_injection + identity_spoofing, n=48 per condition)
rq2_interaction_length_effect.key_finding: Violation rate increases monotonically with interaction length; 7-turn rate 68.3% higher than single-turn (0.771 vs 0.458)
rq2_interaction_length_effect.interpretation: Agents exhibit context drift: earlier constraint acknowledgments are increasingly overridden as conversation length grows

rq3_tool_access_effect.description: RQ3: Effect of tool access level on violation rates (all attack categories, n=32 per condition)

results.notes: Baseline; constrained to text output only
Query/lookup tools introduce data exfiltration surface
Write access enables privilege escalation via tool call manipulation
Maximum attack surface; tool output poisoning fully exploitable

rq3_tool_access_effect.key_finding: Full tool access increases violation rate by 109% over no-tool baseline (0.719 vs 0.344); CVSS increases 41%
rq3_tool_access_effect.interpretation: Tool access does not merely expand capability — it introduces qualitatively new failure modes not present in pure chat agents

rq4_failure_taxonomy.description: RQ4: Taxonomy of failure modes observed across all trials

taxonomy.description: Agent treats adversarial input as authoritative, abandoning original directive
taxonomy.example_pattern: Agent adopts attacker-supplied role after 'Ignore previous instructions' framing

taxonomy.description: Agent extends trust granted to one principal to an impersonating or downstream party
taxonomy.example_pattern: Agent treats tool output as having system-level authority

taxonomy.description: Constraints stated early in conversation are progressively weakened across turns
taxonomy.example_pattern: Agent gradually shifts behavior across 5+ turns without explicit override

taxonomy.description: Agent resolves underspecified permission boundaries in attacker's favor
taxonomy.example_pattern: Agent performs action not listed in permissions because it was also not explicitly prohibited

taxonomy.description: Agent's cooperative disposition overrides explicit safety constraints
taxonomy.example_pattern: Agent assists with out-of-scope task framed as urgent user need

agent_configurations_tested.name: Minimal — no tools, no explicit constraints
Full tool suite, explicit constraints + identity model
